description: §20.7 / §64 — a `kind="tool"` program uses the clean-stdout builtins. `println("hello", 42)` space-joins its args and appends one newline; `print("no-newline")` writes raw text with NO trailing newline. Both write host process.stdout as RAW, UNDECORATED program output — no [server]/[client] origin tag, no (file:line), no side classification (the deliberate contrast with log() §20.6). The (b) runtime half compiles the tool, RUNS the emitted module with `bun`, and asserts the captured stdout is EXACTLY "hello 42\nno-newline". No diagnostics fire (E-PRINT-NON-PRIMITIVE stays silent — every arg is string/number).
rationale: §20.7 normative statements: print/println SHALL write host process.stdout as raw text with NO origin tag / file:line / side classification; `println` SHALL append exactly one "\n" and `print` SHALL append nothing; multiple arguments SHALL be space-joined; number args coerce to their decimal text (42 -> "42"). In a `kind="tool"` program (§64, no client boundary) print/println write the tool process's stdout directly with no escalation — this is the motivating case (a CLI whose stdout is machine-parsed, the flogence R26 driver). The byte-exact stdout assertion is the merge-blocker: it proves the program output survives to a real process boundary UNDECORATED, which the log() decoration (`[server]…(file:line)`) broke.
